metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the deterministic assertion mechanism for `test_enrich_quality`: e.g., each fixture file must include an `expected` block with required keywords/entities, and the assertion checks `all(kw in digest['problem_keywords'] for kw in expected['required_keywords'])`.
findings.finding: AC6's quality criterion 'asserts the digest names the core problem + ≥1 correct entity' is not a specific, deterministic observable outcome — 'names the core problem' requires subjective judgment and the plan does not specify how the pytest assertion will evaluate it (exact keyword match, subset match, pre-labeled expected values in the fixture).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6 (`test_enrich_quality`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fixture schema to the plan: `tests/fixtures/enrich_quality/<id>.json` contains `{"body": "...", "expected": {"required_keywords": [...], "required_entities": [...]}}`. The test asserts every `required_keyword` appears in `problem_keywords` (case-insensitive) and every `required_entity` appears in `key_entities`. This makes the AC deterministic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `category:` value the `enrich.md` prompt should use (likely a new value like `enrichment` is needed, OR confirm which of the four confirmed existing categories is intended and why it is safe for a data-extraction prompt).
findings.finding: The plan instructs 'pick an existing member' from the prompt library's closed category set for `enrich.md`'s `category:` front-matter field, but does not state which one, and none of the confirmed existing members (`review`, `code-review-pass`, `plan-review-criterion`, `plan-review-pass`) is semantically appropriate for a data-extraction enrichment prompt. Choosing the wrong category has behavioral consequences: `category == "review"` makes it a reviewer (appears in `select_reviewers`); `category == "plan-review-criterion"` makes it a plan-review criterion; other values may generate an unknown-category warning or be silently ignored depending on the consuming path.
findings.location: Scope section — 'a `category` value from the prompt library's closed category set (do NOT invent one; pick an existing member)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify WHERE `low_proposition_count` lives: either (a) add it as an optional boolean field to the `ticket_digest` schema and document that it is always present (defaulting false), or (b) define a thin wrapper envelope `{digest: ticket_digest, low_proposition_count: bool}` as the op's return type and update the AC to match.
findings.finding: The `low_proposition_count` flag the plan places 'on the result' when the model returns fewer than min propositions is not defined in the `ticket_digest` schema (`{problem_keywords, component_or_area, key_entities, propositions}`). The mechanism for WHERE this flag lands is unspecified: if it is injected into the validated dict, the JSON Schema does not define it (so it either silently passes if additionalProperties is unset, or fails if it is false); if it is a separate return value the plan's test AC (`test_schema_valid` passes a `ticket_digest`-valid payload) doesn't cover it. The plan also mandates `test_no_nondeterministic_fields`, which is ambiguous when the flag is a deterministic boolean set by a count check.
findings.location: Config-bound threading section — 'keep them and set a `low_proposition_count` flag on the result (never raise)'; Digest schema definition; Testing section — test_propositions_bounded

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `category` value for `enrich.md` (e.g. confirm whether a new non-reviewer category such as `enrichment` needs to be formally added to the closed set, or identify which existing member is the correct fit) — leaving this to implementer inference risks catalog-invariant failures.
findings.finding: The plan instructs the implementer to pick a `category` value for `enrich.md` 'from the prompt library's closed category set (do NOT invent one; pick an existing member)' but never names which member to use. The concrete choice is consequential: if `category: review` is chosen, `prompt.is_reviewer` becomes True and `build_prompt_index` will attempt to include the new prompt in the reviewer catalog, where it enforces 'EXACTLY ONE default reviewer' and 'no dimension collision' invariants (prompts.py:262-301). The four observed categories (`review`, `code-review-pass`, `plan-review-criterion`, `plan-review-pass`) all imply pipeline roles that don't fit a pure enrichment/extraction prompt — none of them is obviously correct, yet the plan leaves the choice to the implementer without guidance.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Prompt `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/enrich.md`, … a `category` value from the prompt library's closed category set (do NOT invent one; pick an existing member)'
findings.suggested_fix: Because none of the four existing categories (`review`, `code-review-pass`, `plan-review-criterion`, `plan-review-pass`) fits a standalone enrichment op, explicitly introduce `category: enrichment` as a new closed-set member and document it — or, if the prompt should remain invisible to the reviewer/criterion pipelines, confirm `category: null` (no category) is the intended choice and note that `is_reviewer` will be False and the prompt will not appear in the reviewer catalog.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `category` value to use in `enrich.md` front-matter (e.g. `transform`) and confirm it will NOT trigger `is_reviewer=True` (which requires `category != 'review'`); the plan currently says 'pick an existing member' without naming one, leaving the executor to guess between two competing closed sets.
findings.finding: The `category` value for `enrich.md` is left to executor guesswork between two competing closed sets in the codebase, and the wrong choice has a silent behavioral consequence.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Prompt `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/enrich.md`, … a `category` value from the prompt library's closed category set (do NOT invent one; pick an existing member)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Prompt front-matter: `category: transform` (the UI palette member closest to a feature-extraction op; ensures `is_reviewer=False` since `prompts.py` derives that from `category == "review"` only).' If `transform` is not the right semantic fit, name whichever existing member is correct.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact return shape of `enrich()` when `low_proposition_count` fires: either add it as an optional (non-required) fifth field in `ticket_digest.schema.json` with `"type": "boolean"`, or define a wrapper dict `{"digest": <ticket_digest>, "low_proposition_count": bool}` — the current plan leaves the executor to invent the shape.
findings.finding: The `low_proposition_count` flag has ambiguous placement: the `ticket_digest` schema has exactly four named fields and the flag is not among them, yet the plan says it is 'set on the result' without specifying whether it is a fifth schema field, a wrapper-dict key alongside the digest, or something else.
findings.location: What section: '…if FEWER than min, keep them and set a `low_proposition_count` flag on the result (never raise)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the schema definition: '`ticket_digest` schema gains an optional fifth field `low_proposition_count: boolean` (not required, default absent/false); the op sets it to `true` when proposition count < min. The `enrich()` function returns a plain `ticket_digest`-valid dict; the flag is part of that dict when set.' This keeps the return type schema-valid and visible to JSON consumers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `category` value for `enrich.md`'s front-matter, or explicitly permit a new category value (e.g. `enrichment`) — the current instruction 'pick an existing member' has no safe resolution since no existing category fits a structured single-turn extraction op.
findings.finding: The prompt front-matter `category` is instructed as 'pick an existing member' from the closed set, but no existing category fits a ticket-enrichment extraction op. The live category set is {'review', 'code-review-pass', 'plan-review-criterion', 'plan-review-pass', 'plan-review-pass'}. Picking 'review' would silently inject 'enrich' into the reviewer catalog (index.json), which enforces an exactly-one-default invariant and no-dimension-collision invariant (prompts.py:297-301) — a catalog-corrupting runtime error if the dimension collides or if a second default is introduced. No existing category is semantically appropriate for a structured single-turn extraction op.
findings.location: Scope section — Prompt `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/enrich.md`, front-matter `category` field
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new category string such as `enrichment` (matching the op's purpose) and document it as the first member of an 'enrichment' category family, OR explicitly state `category: plan-review-pass` if the intent is to have the prompt skipped by the reviewer catalog (non-reviewer ops using this category already exist and are silently ignored by the reviewer index builder).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact `category` value to use in `enrich.md` front-matter (e.g. `category: analysis`) so the executor has a decided value, not a discovery task.
findings.finding: The prompt front-matter requires a `category` value from 'the prompt library's closed category set' but does not enumerate that set or name the required member, forcing the executor to discover it by grepping the codebase — a 'choose an appropriate X' placeholder.
findings.location: Scope (exact edit targets) — Prompt `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/enrich.md` bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Look up the closed category set in `prompts.py` (or wherever the enum is defined), then add a line to the plan: 'Use `category: <exact-value>`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the modeling reference from `completion.py` (which is agentic/findings) to `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` (lines ~157–164) as the canonical example of `RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn")`.
findings.finding: The plan falsely claims `enrich.py` should be 'Modeled on `src/rebar/llm/completion.py` (assemble context → load prompt → `RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn")`)'. Reading `completion.py` shows it builds a `RunRequest` with NO `mode=` or `execution_mode=` fields set at all — it defaults to `mode="findings"` and `execution_mode="agentic"` (a full tool-using agentic verifier). The correct single-turn structured modeling reference is `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` (line ~157–164), which explicitly sets `mode="structured"` and `execution_mode="single_turn"`.
findings.location: What section — 'Modeled on `src/rebar/llm/completion.py` (assemble context → load prompt → `RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn")`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Change the 'What' section to: 'Modeled on `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/__init__.py` (the plan-review single-turn structured path: `RunRequest(mode="structured", execution_mode="single_turn", output_schema=…)`) — NOT on `completion.py`, which is a tool-using agentic verifier using the findings mode, or on `operations.py::review_ticket` for the same reason.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the category tension: either explicitly specify which existing category to use and explain why it is safe (e.g. 'use `plan-review-pass` because build_prompt_index skips non-review prompts and the category is otherwise unused by routing logic'), or acknowledge that a new `enrichment` category is needed and update the plan accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan instructs the implementer to 'pick an existing member' of the prompt library's closed `category` set for `enrich.md`, but no existing category semantically fits a ticket-digest feature extractor. The available categories are: `review` (reviewer catalog, participates in select_reviewers / dimension-collision checks / default-reviewer invariant), `plan-review-criterion`, `plan-review-pass`, `code-review-pass`. Picking `review` would inject `enrich` into the reviewer catalog with an unspecified `dimension`, risking the dimension-collision invariant and exposing the extractor as a selectable reviewer. Picking any other category is semantically incoherent. The plan does not resolve this.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/enrich.md … a `category` value from the prompt library's closed category set (do NOT invent one; pick an existing member)'
findings.suggested_fix: The safest choice is likely `plan-review-pass` or any non-`review` category value, since `build_prompt_index` (prompts.py:273) filters to `is_reviewer` (i.e., `category == 'review'`) and will simply skip the enrich prompt — keeping it out of the reviewer catalog entirely. Specify this explicitly in the plan so the implementer doesn't accidentally pick `review`.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or explicitly defer to a named sibling story with rationale) that exercises `rebar.llm.enrich` end-to-end against the live Anthropic API on at least one real ticket, asserting the returned payload validates against `ticket_digest.schema.json`.
findings.finding: `test_enrich_quality` is placed in `tests/unit/test_enrich.py` and proved only by `python -m pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py -q`; the plan does not state whether this test calls the live LLM or uses a FakeRunner, and no AC exercises the `enrich` op end-to-end against the live Anthropic API — meaning the structured-output parsing, proposition-count post-validation, and flag-setting logic can all be green on mocked output while the real LLM path remains unexercised at ship time.
findings.location: Testing section — 'Quality check as a PYTEST FIXTURE test'; Acceptance Criteria AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: `[ ] Integration smoke: REBAR_LLM_API_KEY=<real> python -m pytest tests/integration/test_enrich_live.py::test_enrich_live -q` which calls the live model on one fixture ticket and asserts `jsonschema.validate(result, ticket_digest_schema)` passes; or explicitly state 'live end-to-end deferred to S2 integration test suite' in the Dependencies section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add ACs (or extend existing ones) covering: (a) LLMConfig.overlap_propositions_min/max readable from env vars, (b) enrich.md front-matter has valid execution_mode and outputs fields, (c) register_contract('ticket_digest', …) is present in contracts.py, (d) `from rebar.llm import enrich` succeeds.
findings.finding: Four described deliverables have no covering acceptance criterion: (1) LLMConfig.overlap_propositions_min/max fields and from_env additions, (2) the enrich.md prompt file (with correct front-matter fields), (3) the contracts.py register_contract('ticket_digest', …) call, and (4) the enrich export from src/rebar/llm/__init__.py. An implementer could skip any of these and all ACs would still pass.
findings.location: Scope section; Acceptance Criteria section
findings.suggested_fix: Add four targeted ACs or extend test_schema_valid to import enrich from rebar.llm and assert register_contract is called. For config: add a test_config_env that sets REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX and verifies LLMConfig.from_env reads them. For the prompt: add a test_prompt_frontmatter that parses enrich.md and asserts execution_mode='single_turn' and outputs='ticket_digest'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `ticket_digest` to `schemas.CONTRACT_SCHEMAS` (or `OUTPUT_SCHEMAS` with an `enrich` key) in `src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py` so the `test_every_schema_file_is_wired` coverage guard passes when `ticket_digest.schema.json` is added.
findings.finding: The `ticket_digest.schema.json` file will be added to `src/rebar/schemas/` but the plan never registers it in `schemas.OUTPUT_SCHEMAS`, `schemas.INPUT_SCHEMAS`, or `schemas.CONTRACT_SCHEMAS` in `src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py`. The `test_every_schema_file_is_wired` coverage-guard test (test_schema_coverage.py:37) mechanically checks that every schema file on disk appears in one of those sets, and will fail immediately on the new file.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Output schema src/rebar/schemas/ticket_digest.schema.json'; src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py CONTRACT_SCHEMAS / OUTPUT_SCHEMAS
findings.suggested_fix: In `src/rebar/schemas/__init__.py`, add `'ticket_digest'` to the `CONTRACT_SCHEMAS` frozenset (it is an internal LLM-op contract schema, not a CLI --output, so CONTRACT_SCHEMAS is the correct bucket — exactly the same pattern as `code_review_base_output` and `reviewer_input`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section documenting the three novel decisions: (1) Cupid single-turn extraction vs. agentic mode, (2) config-bound proposition enforcement via post-validation rather than `register_contract` schema builder, and (3) `low_proposition_count` as a flag (not a raise) — with the rationale for each.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two novel architectural decisions — (1) the Cupid/single-turn structured-extraction pattern (deliberately distinct from the agentic `review_ticket` mode) and (2) the config-bound threading workaround (post-validation instead of schema-builder enforcement) — but does not call for an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update to capture the rationale. Future contributors will not know why the builder lives beside the op, why `register_contract` is not used for bound enforcement, or why this op deliberately diverges from `operations.py::review_ticket`.
findings.location: ## What / ## Config-bound threading (resolves the zero-arg-builder gap) / ## Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief `docs/adr/NNNN-cupid-enrich-op.md` (or append to `CLAUDE.md` under an LLM Ops section) covering: why single-turn structured mode is used, why the zero-arg schema builder cannot carry config-dependent bounds, and why soft degradation (flag) is preferred over hard failure for low proposition counts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability for the two degradation paths: log a WARNING (with ticket_id and count) when `low_proposition_count` is set, and log a WARNING when truncation occurs (>max propositions received); optionally increment a metric counter for each.
findings.finding: The `low_proposition_count` flag and proposition-truncation events are new runtime quality-degradation signals that the plan introduces but does not instrument with any logging, metric, or alert. Operators cannot detect when the LLM is systematically under-producing propositions or when truncation is silently discarding content.
findings.location: ## Config-bound threading / ## Acceptance Criteria – `test_propositions_bounded`
findings.suggested_fix: In `enrich.py`, after post-validation: `if low_proposition_count: logger.warning('enrich: low proposition count', extra={'ticket_id': ticket_id, 'count': len(propositions)})` and similarly for truncation. Wire to the existing `rebar` logger so it flows through whatever log/metric infrastructure the project already uses.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `register_contract("ticket_digest", ...)` is idempotent (e.g., no-op or assert-same-schema on duplicate key), and document the expected behavior when called more than once.
findings.finding: The plan calls `register_contract("ticket_digest", <builder>)` in `contracts.py` but does not specify whether the registry is idempotent under double-registration (e.g., if `enrich.py` is imported more than once or the module is reloaded in a long-running process). Module-level registries are shared mutable state and a second `register_contract` call with the same key could silently overwrite or raise, depending on implementation.
findings.location: Scope section — 'register in `src/rebar/llm/contracts.py` via `register_contract("ticket_digest", <builder>)`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'The `register_contract` call must be idempotent — if the key `"ticket_digest"` is already registered with an identical schema, the call is a no-op; if registered with a different schema, it raises `ValueError`. This prevents double-import issues in test suites and service reloads.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Testing section whether `test_enrich_quality` uses a live LLM call (e.g., guarded by `REBAR_LLM_LIVE_TESTS=1`) or a FakeRunner, and if the latter, explain why canned output faithfully validates extraction quality — otherwise this test is a self-authored-oracle that provides no quality signal.
findings.finding: The 'quality fixture test' (test_enrich_quality) is placed in tests/unit/ and the proving command is a plain pytest invocation, but the test is supposed to assert that the digest 'names the core problem + ≥1 correct entity' over ≥6 independently-labeled tickets. If the test uses FakeRunner (canned/pre-baked LLM output), the assertion is a self-authored-oracle: the fake returns whatever the test author programmed, so the test only verifies the author's own assumptions rather than the LLM's actual extraction quality. The plan does not specify whether test_enrich_quality uses a live LLM call or a fake, leaving the implementation open to the tautology anti-pattern.
findings.location: Testing section / AC: 'test_enrich_quality (≥6 independently-labeled tickets) asserts the digest names the core problem + ≥1 correct entity'
findings.suggested_fix: Mark test_enrich_quality as a live-LLM integration test gated by an env var (e.g., skip unless REBAR_LLM_LIVE_TESTS=1), running it against a real Anthropic call with the ≥6 fixture ticket bodies. The fixture files provide the labeled ground truth; the LLM provides the actual extraction. This preserves fast unit tests in CI while giving a meaningful quality gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-line field-level docstring (or inline comment) to `LLMConfig.overlap_propositions_min/max` explaining that these are *operator-tuning* knobs (not just a schema workaround), and note the S2/S5/S7 downstream consumers so the config surface survives a future Rule-of-Three audit.
findings.finding: The two new LLMConfig fields (overlap_propositions_min/max) and their corresponding env vars/file keys have exactly one consuming call-site (the new enrich op), which is below the Rule-of-Three bar for config-surface additions. The plan's justification — that the zero-arg builder constraint forces bounds out of the schema and into config — is a legitimate constraint, but it should be made explicit that these tunables are intentionally operator-facing (not just an implementation workaround) to survive a future A1 review of the config surface.
findings.location: ## Config-bound threading (resolves the zero-arg-builder gap) / LLMConfig.overlap_propositions_min/max
findings.suggested_fix: In `src/rebar/llm/config.py`, add to the dataclass field: `# Operator-tunable proposition bounds for the enrich op (consumed by S2/S5/S7 overlap detection). The zero-arg contract builder cannot read config, so enforcement lives here rather than in the JSON Schema.`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json` to the plan's edit targets and specify that `regenerate_prompt_index()` must be run (or `index.json` updated) after adding `enrich.md` to the `reviewers/` directory, OR clarify that `enrich.md` should NOT be placed in the packaged `reviewers/` dir (e.g. use `.rebar/prompts/enrich.md`) to avoid triggering the drift gate.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json` is an unenumerated edit target: placing `enrich.md` in `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/` causes `_packaged_prompt_files()` to scan it, and `build_prompt_index()` / `regenerate_prompt_index()` will pick it up — the CI drift gate diffs this derived file. If `enrich.md` uses `category: review` it enters the catalog and the drift gate fails on a stale `index.json`; even with a non-review category the prompt is scanned and `test_prompt_index.py` (which calls `build_prompt_index`) may catch unexpected drift.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Prompt src/rebar/llm/reviewers/enrich.md'
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) add `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/index.json` to Scope edit targets with the note 'run `python -m rebar.llm.prompting.prompts` to regenerate after adding enrich.md'; or (b) state that enrich.md uses a non-`review` category (e.g., `plan-review-pass`) so `build_prompt_index` skips it and the committed index is unchanged — and add `test_prompt_index.py` to the testing section as a test that must still pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope entry for `src/rebar/llm/config.py` that BOTH the `@dataclass` field declarations (`overlap_propositions_min: int = 2`, `overlap_propositions_max: int = 6`) AND the `from_env` resolution lines (`_llm_int(...)`) must be added.
findings.finding: The plan says 'add `overlap_propositions_min/max` to the `from_env` key set' for `LLMConfig` but does not explicitly name adding the corresponding **dataclass field declarations** to the `LLMConfig` dataclass. In the existing code, every config knob that is resolved in `from_env` must also be declared as a field on `@dataclass class LLMConfig` (e.g., `max_tokens`, `max_iterations`, `timeout_s`) — `from_env` passes these to `cls(...)`. The plan's phrasing targets only `from_env`, leaving an edit-target gap for the field declarations.
findings.location: Scope section — 'LLMConfig (`src/rebar/llm/config.py`): add `overlap_propositions_min` (default 2) / `overlap_propositions_max` (default 6) to the `from_env` key set'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Scope bullet to read: 'Config field target: `src/rebar/llm/config.py` — add `overlap_propositions_min: int = 2` / `overlap_propositions_max: int = 6` dataclass fields to `LLMConfig`, and resolve them in `from_env` via `_llm_int(table, cli, "REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_PROPOSITIONS_MIN", "overlap_propositions_min", 2)` / `…MAX…`.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
